info_levels.3: Maybe virtual particles.
Contains relic radiation from primordial universe.
Might be from a magnetar or alien civilization.
Golden record encoded.

info_levels.2: Message: 'This is Linus, and I'm still the maintainer.'
info_levels.3: Probably a tribute signal from Earth.

info_levels.1: Binary data stream, appears like package list.
info_levels.3: Don't run it, might fork bomb the receiver.

info_levels.2: ASCII art: terminal with i3, polybar, and neofetch.
info_levels.3: Transmission includes: 'dotfiles on GitHub'

info_levels.2: Decoding yields: 'THE ANSWER TO THE ULTIMATE QUESTION'
info_levels.3: ... but the data is corrupted. Remains a mystery.
Translated: 'printf("Hello World!\n");' — a programmer's signature?

info_levels.1: Weak signal shaped like a penguin.
info_levels.2: Spectrum resembles logo of Tux, the Linux mascot.
info_levels.3: Message embedded: 'Use Arch btw.'

info_levels.1: Erratic pulses, like a system crash.
info_levels.2: Data contains: 'Kernel panic - not syncing: VFS: Unable to mount root fs'
info_levels.3: Source unknown. Possibly a rogue AI in deep space.
The universe holds its breath.

info_levels.2: Mimics the famous Wow! signal from 1977.
info_levels.3: No origin found. Could be SETI legacy.
Probably transmitted from a free software satellite.

info_levels.2: Ghost echo from a cosmic CDN.
info_levels.3: Message: '404 - Galaxy not found. Retry later.'
Access denied. Insufficient privileges.
Source vanished. Probably a rogue script.
Maybe the universe is a simulation.
SEGFAULT. Core dumped.
You've been rickrolled by deep space.

info_levels.1: Image of a penguin floats in spectrum.
info_levels.3: It's a lone Tux drifting through the void.
No further data. Possibly hoax.
Calculating sigma... no significant detection.

info_levels.1: Pulsar in the Crab Nebula, supernova remnant.
info_levels.2: Period 33 ms. Emits in radio and X-rays.
info_levels.3: Age ~1000 years. Used as a stable time standard.

info_levels.2: Contains a supermassive black hole. Radio lobes extend 1 million ly.
info_levels.3: One of the closest radio galaxies to Earth (12 million ly).

info_levels.2: Distance ~1344 ly. Contains proplyds and young stars.
info_levels.3: Strong water maser emission detected.

info_levels.1: Supermassive black hole at Galactic center.
info_levels.2: Mass ~4 million Suns. Observed in radio and infrared.
info_levels.3: Produces intermittent flares due to accretion.

info_levels.2: First strong black hole candidate discovered.
info_levels.3: Mass ~21 solar masses, accretion disk emits radio.
Rich in star formation, radio continuum from supernova remnants.

info_levels.2: Brightest radio source in the sky outside the Solar system.
info_levels.3: Exploded ~1680, expanding at 5000 km/s.

info_levels.1: Large gamma-ray bubbles above Milky Way.
info_levels.2: Discovered by Fermi telescope, origin uncertain.
info_levels.3: Probably from past AGN activity of Sagittarius A*.

info_levels.1: Galaxy cluster, strong diffuse radio emission.
info_levels.2: Contains a mini-halo and a radio relic.
info_levels.3: Intracluster medium emits thermal X-rays and synchrotron radio.
Jets move at 26% speed of light, produce radio emission.
Has a relativistic jet visible in radio.

info_levels.1: Looks like two digits. Six and Seven.
info_levels.2: Emits 67 in morse code in radio spectre.
info_levels.3: The 67th nebula in a history of astronomy.
